documents.title: Authentication Overview
documents.content: CloudStack API uses API key authentication. Each request must include an 'X-API-Key' header with your API key. API keys can be generated from the CloudStack dashboard under Settings > API Keys. Keys have configurable expiration dates and can be revoked at any time. For production environments, we recommend rotating keys every 90 days.

documents.title: API Key Scopes
documents.content: CloudStack API keys support three permission scopes: 'read' for GET operations only, 'write' for GET, POST, and PUT operations, and 'admin' for full access including DELETE operations and account management. When creating an API key, select the minimum scope required for your use case. Keys with 'admin' scope require two-factor authentication to be enabled on the account.

documents.title: OAuth2 Integration
documents.content: CloudStack supports OAuth2 for enterprise integrations. To configure OAuth2, navigate to Settings > Integrations > OAuth2. Supported grant types include authorization_code for web applications and client_credentials for server-to-server communication. Access tokens expire after 1 hour by default. Refresh tokens are valid for 30 days.

documents.title: Rate Limits Overview
documents.content: CloudStack API has rate limits to ensure fair usage. Standard tier accounts are limited to 100 requests per minute. Professional tier accounts have 500 requests per minute. Enterprise tier accounts have 2000 requests per minute. Rate limits are applied per API key. When you exceed the rate limit, the API returns a 429 Too Many Requests status code.

documents.title: Rate Limit Headers
documents.content: Every API response includes rate limit headers: 'X-RateLimit-Limit' shows your total allowed requests per minute, 'X-RateLimit-Remaining' shows requests remaining in the current window, 'X-RateLimit-Reset' shows the Unix timestamp when the limit resets. Monitor these headers to implement client-side rate limiting and avoid 429 errors.

documents.title: Burst Allowance
documents.content: CloudStack allows short bursts above the rate limit. You can temporarily exceed your rate limit by up to 50% for 10 seconds. This burst allowance helps handle traffic spikes. After a burst, you must stay under your normal rate limit for 30 seconds before another burst is allowed. Sustained abuse of burst limits may result in temporary API suspension.

documents.title: Resources Endpoint
documents.content: The /api/v1/resources endpoint manages cloud resources. GET /resources returns a paginated list of all resources. POST /resources creates a new resource. GET /resources/{id} retrieves a specific resource. PUT /resources/{id} updates a resource. DELETE /resources/{id} removes a resource. All resource operations are logged in the audit trail.

documents.title: Instances Endpoint
documents.content: The /api/v1/instances endpoint manages compute instances. Supported operations include: list instances with filtering, create new instances with custom configurations, start/stop/restart instances, resize instance resources, and terminate instances. Instance creation requires specifying: region, instance_type, image_id, and optionally network_id and security_group_ids.

documents.title: Storage Endpoint
documents.content: The /api/v1/storage endpoint manages block and object storage. Block storage volumes can be created with sizes from 10GB to 16TB. Volumes can be attached to instances, detached, and reattached. Object storage uses S3-compatible API at /api/v1/storage/objects. Maximum object size is 5TB. Multipart upload is required for objects larger than 5GB.

documents.title: Networks Endpoint
documents.content: The /api/v1/networks endpoint manages virtual networks. Create isolated VPCs with custom CIDR blocks (minimum /28, maximum /16). Each VPC supports up to 200 subnets. Network peering between VPCs is supported within the same region. Cross-region peering requires Enterprise tier. Network ACLs and security groups provide layered network security.

documents.title: Error Response Format
documents.content: CloudStack API errors return JSON responses with 'error_code', 'message', and 'details' fields. HTTP status codes indicate error categories: 400 for validation errors, 401 for authentication failures, 403 for permission denied, 404 for resource not found, 429 for rate limiting, 500 for server errors. The 'request_id' field helps support troubleshoot issues.

documents.title: Common Error Codes
documents.content: Common CloudStack error codes: INVALID_API_KEY (401) - API key is invalid or expired, INSUFFICIENT_PERMISSIONS (403) - API key lacks required scope, RESOURCE_NOT_FOUND (404) - requested resource doesn't exist, QUOTA_EXCEEDED (400) - account quota limit reached, RATE_LIMITED (429) - too many requests, INTERNAL_ERROR (500) - server-side error, retry with exponential backoff.

documents.title: Error Handling Best Practices
documents.content: Implement retry logic with exponential backoff for transient errors (5xx status codes and rate limits). Use the request_id when contacting support. Log full error responses for debugging. Validate inputs client-side before API calls to reduce error rates. Set up monitoring for error rate spikes. Consider implementing circuit breakers for critical paths.

documents.title: Pagination Best Practices
documents.content: All list endpoints support cursor-based pagination. Use 'limit' parameter (default 20, max 100) to control page size. The response includes 'next_cursor' for fetching the next page. Always paginate when retrieving large datasets to avoid timeouts. Store cursors if you need to resume pagination later. Cursors are valid for 24 hours.

documents.title: Idempotency
documents.content: For POST and PUT requests, include an 'Idempotency-Key' header with a unique UUID. CloudStack will return the same response for duplicate requests with the same idempotency key within 24 hours. This prevents duplicate resource creation during retries. Always use idempotency keys for critical operations like payments and resource provisioning.

documents.title: Webhook Integration
documents.content: CloudStack supports webhooks for real-time event notifications. Configure webhooks at Settings > Webhooks. Events include: resource.created, resource.updated, resource.deleted, instance.started, instance.stopped, and more. Webhooks use HTTPS with signature verification. Verify signatures using your webhook secret and the X-CloudStack-Signature header.

documents.title: SDK Usage
documents.content: Official CloudStack SDKs are available for Python, JavaScript, Go, Java, and Ruby. SDKs handle authentication, rate limiting, retries, and pagination automatically. Install the Python SDK with 'pip install cloudstack-sdk'. Initialize with: client = CloudStackClient(api_key='your-key'). SDKs are open source and available on GitHub.

documents.title: Pricing Tiers
documents.content: CloudStack offers three pricing tiers: Standard ($99/month) includes 100 req/min rate limit and basic support, Professional ($299/month) includes 500 req/min, priority support, and advanced analytics, Enterprise (custom pricing) includes 2000+ req/min, dedicated support, SLA guarantees, and custom integrations. All tiers include a 14-day free trial.

documents.title: Usage-Based Billing
documents.content: Beyond rate limits, CloudStack charges for resource usage: compute instances are billed per hour based on instance type, storage is billed per GB-month, data transfer is billed per GB (first 100GB/month free), API calls beyond rate limits are $0.001 per request. View detailed usage in the Billing dashboard. Set up billing alerts to avoid surprises.

documents.title: Security Overview
documents.content: CloudStack is SOC2 Type II and ISO 27001 certified. All data is encrypted at rest using AES-256 and in transit using TLS 1.3. API keys are hashed using bcrypt before storage. We perform annual penetration testing and publish results to enterprise customers. Bug bounty program rewards security researchers up to $10,000 for critical vulnerabilities.

documents.title: Network Security
documents.content: CloudStack infrastructure runs in isolated VPCs with no direct internet access. All traffic passes through WAF and DDoS protection. IP allowlisting is available for Enterprise tier to restrict API access to specific IP ranges. VPN connectivity options include IPSec and AWS Direct Connect equivalent. All network traffic is logged for security analysis.

documents.title: Compliance
documents.content: CloudStack maintains compliance with: SOC2 Type II, ISO 27001, GDPR, HIPAA (Enterprise tier with BAA), PCI DSS Level 1. Compliance reports are available in the Trust Center. Data residency options allow you to specify the geographic region for data storage. Annual compliance audits are conducted by independent third parties.

documents.title: Support Channels
documents.content: CloudStack support options: Standard tier gets email support with 48-hour SLA, Professional tier gets email and chat with 24-hour SLA, Enterprise tier gets phone, email, chat with 4-hour SLA for critical issues and dedicated account manager. Community support is available on our Discord server and Stack Overflow (tag: cloudstack-api).

documents.title: Status Page
documents.content: Check service status at status.cloudstack.io. Subscribe to incident notifications via email, SMS, or webhook. The status page shows real-time availability for all API regions, historical uptime (99.95% SLA for Enterprise), planned maintenance windows (announced 7 days in advance), and incident post-mortems published within 5 business days.

documents.title: Available Regions
documents.content: CloudStack is available in 12 regions: us-east-1 (Virginia), us-west-2 (Oregon), eu-west-1 (Ireland), eu-central-1 (Frankfurt), ap-northeast-1 (Tokyo), ap-southeast-1 (Singapore), ap-south-1 (Mumbai), sa-east-1 (São Paulo), ca-central-1 (Montreal), me-south-1 (Bahrain), af-south-1 (Cape Town), ap-southeast-2 (Sydney). Each region has 3 availability zones.

documents.title: Region Selection
documents.content: Choose regions based on: latency to end users, data residency requirements, service availability (some features may be region-specific), and pricing (varies by region). Multi-region deployment is recommended for high availability. Global load balancing can route traffic to the nearest healthy region. Cross-region replication is available for storage.

documents.title: Migration Guide
documents.content: Migrating to CloudStack from AWS: use our migration tool at migrate.cloudstack.io, which maps AWS services to CloudStack equivalents. EC2 instances map to CloudStack Instances, S3 maps to CloudStack Object Storage, RDS maps to CloudStack Managed Databases. The tool generates Terraform configurations for easy migration. Professional services are available for complex migrations.

documents.title: API Compatibility
documents.content: CloudStack offers AWS-compatible APIs for common services. S3-compatible object storage works with existing AWS SDK code by changing the endpoint. CloudStack CLI is compatible with AWS CLI syntax for most commands. Import existing CloudFormation templates with our converter tool. Gradual migration is supported - run hybrid workloads during transition.

documents.title: Metrics and Monitoring
documents.content: CloudStack provides built-in monitoring for all resources. Metrics include: CPU utilization, memory usage, network I/O, disk I/O, and custom application metrics. Metrics are retained for 15 days (Standard), 90 days (Professional), or 1 year (Enterprise). Export metrics to Prometheus, Datadog, or other monitoring tools via our integrations.

documents.title: Alerting
documents.content: Configure alerts based on metric thresholds. Notification channels include email, SMS, Slack, PagerDuty, and webhooks. Alert rules support AND/OR conditions and evaluation windows from 1 minute to 1 hour. Anomaly detection (Enterprise tier) automatically identifies unusual patterns. Alert history is retained for 90 days.

documents.title: Backup and Recovery
documents.content: CloudStack provides automated backups for all managed services. Backup frequency options: daily, weekly, or continuous (point-in-time recovery). Backups are stored in a separate region for disaster recovery. Retention periods: 7 days (Standard), 30 days (Professional), 1 year (Enterprise). Manual snapshots can be taken anytime and retained indefinitely.

documents.title: Disaster Recovery
documents.content: CloudStack supports multiple disaster recovery options: automated failover within a region (3 availability zones), cross-region replication for critical data, pilot light and warm standby configurations for applications. RTO targets: 4 hours (Standard), 1 hour (Professional), 15 minutes (Enterprise). RPO targets: 24 hours, 1 hour, and 5 minutes respectively.

documents.title: API Versioning
documents.content: CloudStack API uses URL-based versioning. Current version is v1 (/api/v1/). Breaking changes trigger new major versions. Minor updates are backwards-compatible and don't change the version. Deprecated features are announced 6 months before removal. Legacy versions are supported for 12 months after a new major version release.

documents.title: Changelog
documents.content: API changelog is published at docs.cloudstack.io/changelog. Subscribe to the developer newsletter for monthly updates. Breaking changes are highlighted in red. New features are marked with release date. Beta features are labeled and may change without notice. SDKs are updated within 1 week of API changes.

documents.title: Account Limits
documents.content: Default account limits: 20 instances (Standard), 100 instances (Professional), unlimited (Enterprise), 10TB storage, 5 VPCs per region, 50 security groups, 100 API keys. Request limit increases via support ticket. Soft limits can be increased instantly. Hard limits require Enterprise tier or special approval.

documents.title: Request Size Limits
documents.content: API request limits: maximum request body size is 10MB, maximum URL length is 8192 characters, maximum query parameters is 100, maximum header size is 32KB. Batch operations are limited to 100 items per request. Large file uploads should use multipart upload or pre-signed URLs for direct upload to storage.

documents.title: Sandbox Environment
documents.content: CloudStack provides a sandbox environment for testing at sandbox.api.cloudstack.io. Sandbox has no rate limits but data is reset daily. Use sandbox API keys (create at dashboard.cloudstack.io/sandbox). Sandbox supports all API endpoints but doesn't provision real resources. Perfect for CI/CD integration tests.

documents.title: Test API Keys
documents.content: Test API keys start with 'test_' and only work in sandbox environment. Test keys have full API access but create mock resources. Use test keys in development and CI pipelines to avoid production costs. Test keys can be created with specific scopes for security testing. Never use production keys in test environments.
